coredata.dc:title: Cu2+ enhanced chemiluminescence of carbon dots-H2O2 system in alkaline solution
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Qiuju
Liu, Guoyong
Yan, Mengxia
Ye, Jing
Zhu, Liping
Huang, Jianshe
Yang, Xiurong
coredata.dc:description: Herein, Chemiluminescence (CL) behaviour of carbon dots (CDs) was investigated by using H2O2 as co-reactant in alkaline solution. The study demonstrated O2 −, OH and 1O2 involved in the CL reaction processes. These reactive oxygen species reacted with CDs to produce CD•+ and CD•-, then the CD•+ reacted with CD•-to generate energy release in the form of CL emission. Importantly, we found that Cu2+ ion could greatly enhanced CL intensity (7.5 times) of CDs-H2O2 system, which was ascribed to Cu2+-catalysed H2O2 decomposition. Based on this system, the CL assay of ascorbic acid (AA) was developed. The limit of detection was as low as 0.03 μM, and the linear range was from 0.1 μM to 100 μM. The proposed method has been successfully utilized to determinate AA in beverage samples with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chemiluminescence
Carbon dots
Cu2+
Ascorbic acid

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM and (B) HR-TEM images of CDs. (C) Survey XPS spectrum of CDs. (D) The deconvolution of C 1s spectrum of CDs.
CL kinetic curves of (a) phloroglucinol-H2O2/NaOH, (b)CDs-NaOH, (c) CDs-H2O2/NaOH, (d) Cu2+-CDs-H2O2/NaOH. Inset: Magnification curves of a.

(A) CL kinetic curves of CDs under oxygen-saturated, air-saturated and nitrogen-saturated. CL kinetic curves of CDs in the absence and presence of different concentrations of (B) p-benzoquinone, (C) thiourea, (D) sodium azide.
Calibration curves for AA detection in Cu2+-CDs-H2O2 CL system (A) and seven replicate detections kinetic curves of CDs system containing 0.5 μM AA within a day (B).

head.ce:title: Cu2+ enhanced chemiluminescence of carbon dots-H2O2 system in alkaline solution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei, Anhui, 230026, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Science and Technology of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei, Anhui 230026, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun, Jilin, 130022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun, Jilin 130022, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemistry, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei, Anhui, 230026, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Science and Technology of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, Chemiluminescence (CL) behaviour of carbon dots (CDs) was investigated by using H2O2 as co-reactant in alkaline solution. The study demonstrated O2 −, OH and 1O2 involved in the CL reaction processes. These reactive oxygen species reacted with CDs to produce CD•+ and CD•-, then the CD•+ reacted with CD•-to generate energy release in the form of CL emission. Importantly, we found that Cu2+ ion could greatly enhanced CL intensity (7.5 times) of CDs-H2O2 system, which was ascribed to Cu2+-catalysed H2O2 decomposition. Based on this system, the CL assay of ascorbic acid (AA) was developed. The limit of detection was as low as 0.03 μM, and the linear range was from 0.1 μM to 100 μM. The proposed method has been successfully utilized to determinate AA in beverage samples with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), have been the subject of extensive research over the past decades due to their inherent advantages, such as facile fabrication, excellent biocompatibility, outstanding energy and charge transfer properties [1]. CDs are widely used in sensors, bioimaging, photovoltaic devices, electrochemical catalysis and other fields [2–5]. Up to now, there are many reports on the application of CDs in photoluminescence (PL) and electrochemiluminescence (ECL) sensing [6–8]. Carbon dots are also used for chemiluminescence (CL) detection, where they are directly used luminescent reagents or as catalysts for other CL system. For example, Lin's group reported fluorescent CDs reacted with classical oxidants, such as acidic potassium permanganate and cerium (IV) to produce chemiluminescence [9]. Then, the radiative recombination of oxidant-injected holes and electrons in the CDs-NaIO4-H2O2 CL system was found [10]. Zhang's group observed strong CL emission of CDs in a strong alkaline solution [11]. In other applications, Li's group found that carbon dots could be used as the catalyst in CL of luminol system [12]. Although some CL systems have been developed due to the advantages of carbon dots, the CL intensity of the reaction needs to be further enhanced for more sensitive CL detection. It has been reported triethylamine could increase the CL intensity of carbon dots/K2S2O8 by 20 times [13]. Although chemiluminescence detection has the advantages of simple instruments, low interference from background scattering light and no complex electrode modification steps, the study of CL system based on carbon dots is far from sufficient, and there is still a great demand for further study. Therefore, it is very meaningful to synthesize new highly efficient CL CDs materials and to develop new strategy for enhancement of CL intensity of CDs systems.

Transition metals and transition metals-based composites have been extensively studied for the enhancement of organic small molecules chemiluminescence. Cu (II), Co (II) ion and their nanomaterials could enhance the CL intensity via catalysing Fenton-like reaction to accelerate decomposition of hydrogen peroxide. Therefore, they have been used in H2O2-based CL system. For example, Cu2+ functionalized gold nanoparticle has been used in luminol derivatives-H2O2 system for the detection of pyrophosphate ion [14]. Fu's group reported self-enhancement chemiluminescence of Cu (II) ethylenediaminetetraacetate chelate in luminol-H2O2 system [15]. However, only a few researches have been done on the CL enhancement of carbon dots by transition metals, although there are many fluorescence studies and applications of electron/energy transfer and interaction between transition metal ions and fluorescent carbon dots [16]. Na's group studied CL intensity of ozone oxidized-carbon nanodots with different metal ions in the system, and based on this, developing a new method for rapid detection of metal ions [17]. The strong CL signals of carbon nanodots system was also observed by the catalysis of Co (II)-triggered Fenton-like reaction [18]. Therefore, further study on transition metal ions in the CL system of carbon dots is of great significance for the practical application.

In this work, we investigated the CL behaviour of CDs under strong alkaline solution with hydrogen peroxide as a co-reactant. We found that Cu2+ ion could dramatically enhance the CL intensity of CDs-H2O2 system, which could be increased by 7.5 times. Based on this phenomenon, we developed a new CL method to detect ascorbic acid, which further verified the reaction mechanism.

CDs were synthesized by a simple solvothermal method by using phloroglucinol as precursor (Fig. S1, ESI †) [19]. The XRD pattern of the prepared CDs, shown in Fig. S2 (ESI †), demonstrated a broad characteristic peak (0 0 2) at about 22°, suggesting CDs showed graphite-like structures. Another characteristic peak at 11° indicates the partial oxidation of CDs [20]. The morphology of CDs was investigated using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 1 A, CDs show an irregular shape with an average size of 5.4 nm (Fig. S3, ESI †). As shown in Fig. 1B, HR-TEM (high-resolution transmission electron microscopy) image of CDs shows an obvious crystal lattice with the lattice parameter of 0.21 nm corresponding to the (1 0 0) crystal plane of the graphite [7,19]. The elemental composition of the obtained CDs was characterized by the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Fig. 1C), proving the existence of C and O element (Table S1, ESI †). The high resolution XPS spectrum for C1s is deconvoluted into four peaks at 284.6 eV, 286 eV, 288.4 eV and 289.2 eV, which can be attributed to C–C, C–O, CO and O–CO, respectively (Fig. 1D) [12,21,22]. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum of CDs (Fig. S4, ESI †) shows the strong stretching vibration bands of OH, CO, CC, and C–O at 3415, 1713, 1604, and 1141 cm−1, respectively, which is consistent with XPS analysis. Compared with phloroglucinol (Fig. S4, ESI †), the band of CO group is obviously observed on FT-IR spectrum of CDs, demonstrating the formation of CDs. To further characterize the properties of carbon dots, the UV–vis absorption spectroscopy measurement was performed. As shown in Fig. S5 (ESI †), the peak at about 290 nm is assigned to the π-π* transition of the conjugation structure within CDs. The n–π* transition peak at about 364 nm was also observed [23]. The fluorescence (FL) maximum excitation wavelength of CDs is about 480 nm, and the FL maximum emission is at about 540 nm (Fig. S6, ESI †). With the increase of excitation wavelength, the FL emission wavelength has a very small shift (Fig. S7, ESI †), indicating that the synthesized CDs are uniform in the size and the surface state [24].

Furthermore, the CL property of the CDs was investigated in detail. As shown in Fig. 2 , weak CL emission was observed when carbon dots were added into a strong alkaline solution (curve b). In contrast, there was no obvious CL signal for the precursor solution of CDs (curve a), indicating that CDs were the CL emitter in this system. When hydrogen peroxide is added as a co-reactant, the CL signal is enhanced by 2.5 times (curve c). Additionally, the CL intensity is about 7.5 times higher after adding Cu2+ ion into CDs-H2O2/NaOH solution (curve d). These results clearly demonstrated that Cu2+ ion could greatly increase the CL signal of CDs-H2O2 system. To get the optimal CL activity, the effect of the pH of H2O2 solution, the concentration of H2O2, the different metal ions and the concentration of Cu2+ was studied (Fig. S8, ESI †). The results showed that when 100 mM H2O2 in 0.1 M NaOH solution as a co-reactant and 0.1 mM of Cu2+ was used, the highest CL signal was obtained for Cu2+-CDs-H2O2 system. It can be seen that some other transition metal ions could also have enhancing effect on CL intensity of CDs-H2O2 system, and the highest enhancement was obtained by the addition of Cu2+ ions.

The high-energy cut off filters of various wavelengths were used to determine the CL spectrum to identify the emitting species in the CL reaction. The maximum emission peak located at 550 nm (Fig. S9 ESI †), which is similar to the FL spectrum of the CDs. Therefore, the CL can be attributed to the excited CDs. To explain the CL phenomenon, O2, N2 and radical scavengers were also used to investigate the CL mechanism of CDs. In Fig. 3 A, the CL intensity decreases in a nitrogen-saturated solution and increased in an oxygen-saturated solution, indicating that the dissolved oxygen (O2) participated in the CL reaction. In addition, to identify the radical species, the radical scavengers of p-benzoquinone, thiourea and NaN3 were used to specially scavenge O2 −, OH and 1O2, respectively. It can be seen that the CL intensity obviously decreases with increasing concentrations of p-benzoquinone (Fig. 3B), thiourea (Fig. 3C) and NaN3(Fig. 3D). These results demonstrated O2 −, OH and 1O2 involved in the CL reaction processes. For CDs-H2O2 system, H2O2 decomposes to produce hydroxyl radicals, which reacts with ethanol and CDs to form intermediate of CD + [25]. O2 − and CD• + were also formed by electron transfer process from CDs to dissolved oxygen in concentrated sodium hydroxide solution. Then, the electron was donated by O2 − to CDs and CDs with a negative charge (CD• -) produced. The CD + reacted with CD•-to release energy in the form of CL emission [10]. In addition, O2 − can reacts with O2 − or OH to form intermediate of 1O2 [26], which can also influence the CL reaction. For example, it has been reported that the CL spectrum of the 1O2 overlaps the excitation wavelength for CDs, which leads to direct energy transfer from 1O2* to the CDs [25,27]. Therefore, 1O2 may further enhance the chemiluminescence of CDs-H2O2 chemiluminescence. The mechanism of Cu2+-enhanced CL intensity has been studied in recent year. For Cu2+-CDs-H2O2 system, Cu2+ can catalyse H2O2 to produce OH and O2 − similarly to Fenton reaction, which accelerates the production of reactive oxygen species. The produced free radicals can interact with each other and/or with CDs [28,29]. Therefore, significant enhancement of CL signal was observed. Thus, Cu2+ catalyses H2O2 to release oxygen-associated free radical, which reacted with CDs and accelerated the electron–hole annihilation to produce energy release in the form of CL emission.

Ascorbic acid (AA) or vitamin C, as a food additive, is the most important water-soluble antioxidant present in food and beverages. Furthermore, AA has a positive effect on cancer prevention, cardiovascular disease prevention and immunity improvement, involved in many metabolic pathways such as iron absorption, collagen synthesis and immune response activation [30,31]. Hence, developing a sensitive and convenient method for AA assays is of huge significance. AA is a common reductant, and is often used as a free radical scavenger to OH and O2 −. The inhibition of ascorbic acid on the CL of Cu2+-CDs-H2O2 system verified the CL mechanism of the reaction once again (Fig. S10, ESI †). Based on this, we developed a CL method to detect AA. The calibration curve for the determination of AA is shown in Fig. 4 A. The excellent linear response has achieved between the logarithm of the concentration of AA from 0.1 μM to 100 μM and the change of CL intensity (I0 − I). The linear regression equation ΔI = 50876.9 + 47475 log C (unit of C is μM, r = 0.998) has obtained. The relative CL intensity ΔI = I0 − I is the difference between the CL intensity of Cu2+-CDs-H2O2 system in the absence of AA (I0) and in presence of AA (I). The limit of detection (LOD) for AA is 0.03 μM (LOD is defined as the concentration corresponding to 3σ, σ is the standard deviation of blank). Compared with the previously reported methods, the proposed CL method shows lower limit of detection, and comparable linear range (Table S2). The effects of co-existing small molecules and ions on the detection of AA (1 μM) was investigated. As shown in Fig. S11 (ESI †), 100-fold excess of K+, Na+, NO3 −, 10-fold of L-alanine, L-serine, L-histidine, L-glycine and glucose, and 5-fold of L-cysteine and glutathione all show low interference with AA detection. The results indicate the designed CL method has a good selectivity for AA detection. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was obtained to be 1.88% (Fig. 4B) for seven replicate determinations of 0.5 μM AA within a day. This result indicated good repeatability of the proposed method. The chemiluminescence stability of the as-prepared carbon dots was evaluated in 21 days after the synthesis of materials. And CL intensity of CDs is basically stable in the first 7 days with RSD of 2.7% (n = 7), and which decreased to 89% of the initial intensity after 21 days, indicating the prepared CDs had a good long-term stability (Fig. S12 ESI †). To evaluate the applicability of the proposed method in practical samples, beverage samples were spiked with AA standard solutions and determined with the proposed method. Before the measurement, the beverage was repeated centrifugation to remove sediment. The recovery of 103.7%–105.4% was obtained (Table S3), demonstrating its reliability and application potential in real sample analysis.

In summary, a simple strategy for preparing the CDs with intense CL emission was developed. The as-prepared CDs produced highly efficient CL emission with H2O2 as co-reaction in a strong alkaline solution. The CL emission mechanism of CDs-H2O2 system was studied in detail, and the radiative recombination of hole-injected and electron-injected CDs to release energy in the form of CL emission was proposed. Importantly, we found that Cu2+ could greatly enhanced the CL intensity of CDs-H2O2 system, which was ascribed to the catalytic activity of Cu2+ towards the decomposition of H2O2 to release OH and O2 −. The highly efficient CL emission of this system is beneficial for its application in sensitive CL detection. A new analytical method for the determination of AA was developed based on its inhibition of Cu2+-CDs-H2O2 system CL emission. The proposed method exhibits high sensitivity and good selectivity, and can be applied to the determination of AA in beverage samples. This work presented here may be extended to develop other new CDs-based CL system, which will find extensive applications for bioanalysis.
The authors declare no competing financial interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2016YFA0201301), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21435005, 21627808), and Key Research Program of Frontier Sciences, CAS (No. QYZDY-SSW-SLH019).
